title: The Market Hall at Chipping Campden
summary: Built in 1627 by Sir Baptist Hicks, the Market Hall sheltered traders selling butter, cheese, and poultry. It remains one of the finest examples in the Cotswolds.
body: The Market Hall was built in 1627 by the wealthy merchant Sir Baptist Hicks as a shelter for market traders. Its open arches allowed goods — mainly butter, cheese, and poultry — to be sold in all weathers. Hicks, who made his fortune in the London silk trade, was a great benefactor to the town. The Hall was given to the National Trust in 1902 and remains one of the most photographed buildings in the Cotswolds. It stands at the start of the Cotswold Way, marking the beginning of many walkers' journeys south to Bath.

title: The Cotswold Wool Trade
summary: The golden stone buildings you see across the Cotswolds were funded by medieval wool wealth. Cotswold sheep produced the finest fleece in Europe.
body: Between the 13th and 16th centuries, the Cotswolds was one of the wealthiest regions in England. Cotswold Lion sheep produced fine, heavy fleeces that were exported across Europe. The 'wool churches' built with this wealth — at Chipping Campden, Northleach, and Cirencester — are among the finest parish churches in England. The honey-coloured limestone buildings that define the Cotswold landscape were largely built during this prosperous era. Even the name 'Chipping' comes from the Old English 'ceaping', meaning market.

title: Broadway Tower: A Folly with a View
summary: Built in 1798 for Lady Coventry, who wanted to know if a beacon on this hilltop could be seen from her home 22 miles away. It could.
body: Broadway Tower was designed by James Wyatt and built in 1798 for Lady Coventry of Croome Court in Worcestershire. She wanted to know whether a beacon lit on this hilltop — the second highest point in the Cotswolds — could be seen from her home 22 miles away. It could. The tower has since served as a retreat for William Morris and the Arts and Crafts movement, a Cold War nuclear bunker observation post, and is now a popular tourist attraction. On a clear day, 16 counties are visible from the top.

title: Broadway: Artists' Village
summary: In the 1880s, Broadway became a colony for American and British artists, drawn by the golden light and picturesque cottages.
body: In the 1880s, Broadway became an artists' colony when the American painter Francis Davis Millet settled there. He was soon joined by John Singer Sargent, Edwin Austin Abbey, and other leading artists of the day. The village's wide, tree-lined main street, honey-coloured buildings, and soft Cotswold light made it irresistible. Sargent painted his famous 'Carnation, Lily, Lily, Rose' in the garden of Farnham House here. The tradition continues today with the Gordon Russell Design Museum and numerous galleries.

title: Stanway's Gravity Fountain
summary: Stanway House has the tallest gravity-fed fountain in the world at 300 feet, powered entirely by the natural fall of water from the hillside above.
body: Stanway House, a magnificent Jacobean manor, is home to the tallest gravity-fed fountain in the world. Rising to 300 feet, the fountain is powered entirely by a natural water supply from the hills above — no pumps needed. The house has been in the same family, the Earls of Wemyss, since 1533. Its gatehouse is considered one of the finest in England, and the tithe barn dates from the 14th century. The cricket ground in the grounds is one of the most scenic in the country.

title: Winchcombe: Capital of Mercia
summary: In Saxon times, Winchcombe was the capital of its own shire and home to a great abbey. Today, traces of this ancient importance survive in its street plan.
body: Winchcombe was once the capital of the Anglo-Saxon kingdom of Winchcombeshire, with a powerful Benedictine abbey founded in 798 AD. The abbey was demolished during the Dissolution of the Monasteries under Henry VIII, but its stones were reused in buildings throughout the town. Nearby Sudeley Castle was the home and burial place of Katherine Parr, the last wife of Henry VIII. The town retains its medieval street plan and is celebrated for its walking festival each May.

title: Cleeve Common: Wild Hilltop
summary: Cleeve Common is the largest area of unimproved limestone grassland in Gloucestershire, home to rare orchids, skylarks, and ancient earthworks.
body: At 1,083 acres, Cleeve Common is the largest area of common land in Gloucestershire and a Site of Special Scientific Interest. The unimproved limestone grassland supports rare wildflowers including bee orchids, pyramidal orchids, and frog orchids. Skylarks nest on the open hillside and buzzards ride the thermals along the escarpment edge. The hilltop also contains Bronze Age ring cairns and an Iron Age cross-dyke. At 330 metres, this is the highest point on the entire Cotswold Way.

title: The Devil's Chimney
summary: This natural limestone pillar was left standing after quarrying. Legend says the devil rises from it on dark nights to sit on the hilltop.
body: The Devil's Chimney is a limestone pillar standing on Leckhampton Hill, created as a remnant of the extensive quarrying that took place from the 18th to early 20th century. The quarries supplied stone for much of Regency Cheltenham below. Local legend says the devil emerges from the chimney on dark nights to sit on the hilltop. In reality, the pillar has been reinforced with concrete and iron rods to prevent collapse. It has been climbed many times, most famously by a group of students in the 1980s who placed a traffic cone on top.

title: The 99 Yew Trees of Painswick
summary: Legend holds that there can never be more than 99 yew trees in Painswick churchyard — the devil always kills the hundredth.
body: St Mary's churchyard in Painswick is famous for its 99 clipped yew trees, many of which were planted in the 18th century. Local legend insists that there can never be 100 trees — whenever a hundredth is planted, the devil kills it. The reality is less dramatic: the count varies depending on how you define a separate tree. The churchyard also contains remarkable 17th and 18th-century table tombs carved by local masons, considered among the finest in England. The annual Clipping Ceremony (from 'ycleping', meaning embracing) sees the church encircled by parishioners holding hands.

title: Standish Wood: Ancient Woodland
summary: This ancient woodland has been continuously wooded since at least the medieval period. In spring, the forest floor erupts in a carpet of bluebells.
body: Standish Wood is classified as ancient semi-natural woodland, meaning it has been continuously wooded since at least 1600 AD and likely much longer. Dominated by beech trees, it sits on the Cotswold escarpment with dramatic views west. In late April and May, the woodland floor is carpeted with bluebells — one of the finest displays in Gloucestershire. The wood is also home to fallow deer, badgers, and a variety of woodland birds including nuthatches, treecreepers, and great spotted woodpeckers.

title: Nympsfield Long Barrow
summary: Near Coaley Peak lies Nympsfield Long Barrow, a Neolithic burial chamber dating from around 2800 BC — older than Stonehenge.
body: Just a short walk from Coaley Peak viewpoint lies Nympsfield Long Barrow, a Neolithic chambered tomb dating from around 2800 BC. When excavated, it contained the remains of over 20 individuals along with pottery and animal bones. The tomb was originally covered by a mound of earth and stone nearly 30 metres long. Today the stone chambers are exposed and freely accessible. Nearby Hetty Pegler's Tump, another long barrow, can also be explored with a torch. These monuments predate Stonehenge and the Egyptian pyramids.

title: Bath Abbey: Journey's End
summary: Bath Abbey has stood on this site since the 7th century. The current building, begun in 1499, is the last great medieval church built in England.
body: Bath Abbey, where many Cotswold Way walkers celebrate completing their 102-mile journey, has been a place of worship since the 7th century. The present building was begun in 1499 by Bishop Oliver King, who had a dream of angels ascending and descending a ladder to heaven — depicted on the west front. It is the last great medieval church built in England. The Abbey sits above the ancient Roman Baths, the only naturally occurring hot springs in the British Isles. The waters rise at 46°C from a depth of over 2,700 metres.
